summary: Gruber reviews the Apple Watch Series 5, arguing that its always-on display marks the moment Apple Watch finally hits its stride. He frames this through a broader theory of product evolution: new products ship with obvious flaws, then iterate until those flaws are resolved. The always-on display was his single biggest complaint since the original Apple Watch, and he compares its significance to the iPhone's retina display moment. He notes battery life remains adequate despite the always-on feature, and dismisses other Series 5 additions like the compass as minor. For Gruber, the always-on display alone justified the upgrade.
key_points: New products hit their stride not when they become popular, but when their glaring flaws are mostly resolved — for Apple Watch, that's Series 5
The original Apple Watch shipped perhaps slightly too early, driven by pressure to prove Apple could launch a great post-Jobs product
The always-on display solves Gruber's biggest complaint as a lifelong watch wearer who couldn't accept glancing at a blank screen
Battery life with always-on display is more than adequate for daily use, removing the obvious concern about the feature
Apple Watch is already more successful than the iPod ever was, just four years in
money_quotes: The right time to ship a new product is when its utility and appeal outweigh its obvious flaws.
And then they did what Apple does: iterate, iterate, iterate.
When I talk about a product hitting its stride, I don't mean that's when it became popular — I mean that's when the big missing pieces are mostly filled in.
Four years later, I'm still not used to it.
To me, the always-on display is the Apple Watch's retina display moment — once you see it, you can't go back.
I wouldn't want to spend a single day wearing this watch with the always-on display turned off.
I'd have placed an order for a Series 5 watch even if Apple had put up a slide that said 'Just one new feature: Always-on display.'
key_insight: The always-on display transforms Apple Watch from a gadget that happens to tell time into a proper watch, marking the moment the product's most fundamental flaw is finally resolved.
